lessons.title: Electric Charge: Push and Pull
lessons.content:
Have you ever pulled a sock out of the dryer and had it cling to a shirt? Or felt your hair stand up after tugging off a wool hat? That little tug is **electric charge** at work — the same basic thing behind lightning, batteries, and (later in this course) radio and light.

**What is charge?** Everyday stuff is built from tiny particles. Some carry a property we call *negative* charge (electrons), and some carry *positive* charge (protons). Most objects have equal amounts, so they feel "neutral" — no net push. But rub two materials together and a few electrons hop from one to the other. Now one object has extra negative charge and the other has extra positive. We label them with a minus sign ($-$) and a plus sign ($+$).

**The one rule to remember:** charges push and pull on each other without touching.

- **Like charges repel** — two pluses push apart; two minuses push apart.
- **Opposite charges attract** — a plus and a minus pull together.

That is why a charged balloon sticks to a wall, and why static-clingy clothes grab each other. The pull or push is called the **electric force**.

One more everyday fact: the force gets **weaker as the charges move farther apart**, and stronger as they get closer. Hold the balloon near the wall and the tug is strong; pull it away and the tug fades.

**Recap:** charge comes in two kinds, plus and minus. Same kinds repel, opposite kinds attract, and the force weakens with distance. That simple push-and-pull is the seed of everything else in this topic.

items.prompt: Electric charge comes in how many kinds, and what are they called?
choices: One kind, called neutral
Two kinds, called positive and negative
Three kinds, called positive, negative, and neutral
Four kinds, one for each direction
items.explanation: There are exactly two kinds of charge: positive (written +) and negative (written -). 'Neutral' is not a third kind of charge — it just means an object has equal amounts of + and -, so they cancel out.

items.prompt: Two objects both carry extra positive charge. What do they do to each other?
choices: They pull together (attract)
They push apart (repel)
They ignore each other
They swap their charges
items.explanation: Like charges repel. Two positives (or two negatives) push apart. Only opposite charges — one + and one - — attract.

items.prompt: A positively charged balloon is brought near a negatively charged spot on a wall. What happens?
choices: They repel, pushing the balloon away
They attract, pulling the balloon toward the wall
Nothing, because one is on a wall
The balloon loses all its charge instantly
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract. A positive balloon and a negative patch pull toward each other, which is exactly why a rubbed balloon sticks to a wall.

items.prompt: You slowly move two attracting charges farther apart. How does the force between them change?
choices: It gets stronger
It gets weaker
It stays exactly the same
It reverses and becomes a push
items.explanation: The electric force weakens with distance. Closer charges feel a stronger pull or push; farther charges feel a weaker one. Moving apart never flips attraction into repulsion — only the sign of the charges does that.

items.prompt: Why is an ordinary, untouched object usually 'neutral'?
choices: It contains no charged particles at all
It has equal amounts of positive and negative charge, which cancel out
It only contains positive charge
Its charges are frozen and cannot move
items.explanation: Neutral does not mean empty of charge. It means the positive charge (protons) and negative charge (electrons) are present in equal amounts, so there is no leftover push or pull.

items.prompt: When you rub a wool hat on your hair and your hair stands up, what has happened?
choices: Charge was created from nothing by the rubbing
Some electrons moved from one material to the other, leaving each with a net charge
The hair became heavier
The hat turned the hair into a magnet
items.explanation: Rubbing transfers electrons between the materials. One ends up with extra negative charge and the other with extra positive. Charge is moved, not made from nothing. The standing hair is like charges repelling each other.

items.prompt: A plus sign (+) on an object means it has, compared to normal:
choices: Extra electrons
A shortage of negative charge (it lost some electrons)
Extra weight
No charge at all
items.explanation: A + label means the object is missing some of its negative charge (it gave up electrons), so positive charge is left over. A - label means the opposite: extra electrons.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation is the clearest example of the electric force?
choices: A heavy box resting on the floor
A sock clinging to a shirt straight out of the dryer
A ball rolling down a hill
Water boiling in a pot
items.explanation: Static cling is the electric force in action: rubbing in the dryer leaves the fabrics oppositely charged, so they attract and stick. The other examples are gravity, motion, and heat — not electric charge.

items.prompt: Two charges feel a push without ever touching. Is that possible?
choices: No, objects must touch to affect each other
Yes — charges push and pull on each other across the empty space between them
Only if the air is humid
Only if both charges are negative
items.explanation: The electric force acts at a distance, across the space between charges, with no contact needed. (How that 'reach across space' works is the idea of a field, coming up in the next lesson.)

lessons.title: What Is a Field? The Electric Field
lessons.content:
In the last lesson, charges pushed and pulled across empty space without touching. How does one charge 'reach' another that is far away? The answer is one of the most useful ideas in all of physics: the **field**.

**What is a field?** A field just means *a quantity that has a value at every point in space*. Think of the temperature in a room: every spot has some temperature — warmer near the heater, cooler by the window. You could, in principle, write a number at every point. That map of values is a *temperature field*. Or picture the warmth filling the space around a campfire: strong up close, faint far away. A field is that kind of thing — a value spread through space around a source.

**The electric field.** A charge fills the space around it with an **electric field**: at every point, the field tells you which way another charge would be pushed, and how hard. We can draw it with **field lines** — arrows that show the direction of the push.

- Around a **positive** charge, the field lines point **outward** (a stray positive charge would be pushed away).
- Around a **negative** charge, the field lines point **inward** (a stray positive charge would be pulled in).

Two things to read from a field-line picture:

1. **Direction** — which way the arrows point.
2. **Strength** — where the lines are crowded together, the field is **strong**; where they spread apart, it is **weak**. So the field is strongest right next to the charge and gets **weaker with distance** — the same fading we saw with the force.

The charge is the *source*; the field is the influence it spreads around itself. A charge dropped into that field then feels a push — that push is the electric force from Lesson 1.

**Recap:** a field is a value at every point in space. An electric charge surrounds itself with an electric field that points out of plus and into minus, and that is strong nearby and weak far away.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a 'field'?
choices: A flat grassy area
A quantity that has a value at every point in space
A single number that describes a whole object
A wire that carries electricity
items.explanation: A field is a quantity with a value at every point in space — like temperature throughout a room. The electric field assigns a push (direction and strength) to every point around a charge.

items.prompt: Which everyday example is most like a field?
choices: The temperature at every spot in a room
The total number of chairs in a room
The color of a single chair
The price of the room
items.explanation: Temperature has a value at every point in the room — warmer near the heater, cooler by the window. That 'value at every point' is exactly what a field is. A single total or one object's color is just one number, not a value spread through space.

items.prompt: Which way do electric field lines point around a single positive charge?
choices: Outward, away from the charge
Inward, toward the charge
In circles around the charge
They do not point any particular way
items.explanation: Field lines point out of a positive charge, showing that another positive charge placed nearby would be pushed away. Around a negative charge the lines point inward instead.

items.prompt: In a field-line drawing, what does it mean where the lines are crowded close together?
choices: The field is weak there
The field is strong there
There is no field there
The charge is negative there
items.explanation: Crowded (closely spaced) field lines mean a strong field; widely spread lines mean a weak field. Lines are most crowded right next to the charge, so the field is strongest there.

items.prompt: As you move farther away from a charge, the electric field around it:
choices: Gets stronger
Gets weaker
Stays the same everywhere
Disappears completely at a fixed edge
items.explanation: The field gets weaker with distance — the field lines spread out as you go farther away. It fades gradually rather than stopping at a hard edge.

items.prompt: What creates an electric field in the first place?
choices: Electric charge
Heat
Sound
Gravity
items.explanation: An electric charge is the source of an electric field; it fills the surrounding space with the field. (A moving charge also makes a magnetic field — that comes in a later lesson.)

items.prompt: Does an electric field exist in the space around a lone charge even if no other charge is nearby to feel it?
choices: No — the field appears only when a second charge shows up
Yes — the charge fills the space with its field whether or not anything is there to feel it
Only if the charge is positive
Only if the charge is touching something
items.explanation: The field is set up by the source charge throughout the surrounding space, present whether or not a second charge is there. A push (force) only appears when another charge is placed in that field.

items.prompt: The field lines around a negative charge point inward. What does that tell you?
choices: A nearby positive charge would be pushed away
A nearby positive charge would be pulled toward the negative charge
The negative charge has no field
Nothing can be read from the direction
items.explanation: Inward-pointing lines mean a stray positive charge would be pulled in toward the negative charge — opposite charges attract. The arrow direction is the direction a positive test charge would be pushed.

items.prompt: How is the campfire analogy a good picture of a field?
choices: The fire's warmth has a value at every point around it — strong up close, faint far away
The fire is a single point with no surroundings
The warmth is the same at every distance
The warmth only exists if you touch the flames
items.explanation: The warmth around a fire has a value at every point in the space around it, strongest nearby and fading with distance — just like a field. An electric field surrounds a charge in the same spread-through-space way.

lessons.title: Voltage and the Energy in a Field
lessons.content:
We have an electric field that pushes charges around. Pushing something takes effort, and effort means **energy**. This lesson connects the field to two everyday words: **voltage** and **energy**.

**Voltage as 'electrical height'.** Imagine a hill. A ball at the top can roll down; a ball at the bottom just sits there. Height tells you how much push is available. **Voltage** (also called *electric potential*) is the electrical version of height. A point at high voltage is like the top of a hill for a positive charge; a point at low voltage is the bottom. Just as a ball rolls downhill, a positive charge tends to move from high voltage to low voltage. The difference in 'electrical height' between two points is what a battery provides: a 9-volt battery keeps one terminal 9 units of electrical height above the other. Voltage is measured in **volts** (V).

**A gentle note on field strength.** How strong an electric field is at a point can be described as how steeply the 'electrical height' changes there — a steep drop means a strong field. The strength of an electric field is measured in **volts per meter** (V/m), which you can read as simply 'how strong the push is' over a given distance. You do not need any formula — just the idea: steeper change in voltage means a stronger field.

**Energy stored in a field.** Here is a key idea for the whole course: *a field holds energy*. The very space where a field exists is loaded with energy, like a stretched spring or water held behind a dam. The stronger the field, the more energy packed into that space. This is why a charged-up capacitor (a small component that stores charge) can deliver a spark, and — looking ahead — it is why electromagnetic waves can carry energy across a room, or across the galaxy: they carry their fields, and the fields carry energy.

**Recap:** voltage is electrical 'height,' measured in volts; positive charges move from high to low voltage. Field strength (volts per meter) says how strong the push is. And crucially, a field stores energy — the stronger the field, the more energy in that space.

items.prompt: Voltage is best thought of as the electrical version of what everyday idea?
choices: Weight
Height (like the height of a hill)
Color
Temperature
items.explanation: Voltage is like height. A point at high voltage is like the top of a hill, and a positive charge tends to 'roll downhill' from high voltage to low voltage, just as a ball rolls down a slope.

items.prompt: Voltage is measured in:
choices: Volts
Meters
Seconds
Degrees
items.explanation: Voltage (electric potential) is measured in volts (V). Meters measure distance, seconds measure time, and degrees measure angle or temperature.

items.prompt: Following the hill analogy, which way does a positive charge tend to move on its own?
choices: From low voltage to high voltage
From high voltage to low voltage
It stays put no matter what
Sideways, along the same voltage
items.explanation: Like a ball rolling downhill, a positive charge tends to move from high voltage ('high ground') to low voltage ('low ground'). Pushing it the other way, uphill, takes added energy.

items.prompt: What does a 9-volt battery provide between its two terminals?
choices: A difference in 'electrical height' of 9 volts
Exactly 9 electrons
A temperature of 9 degrees
A magnetic field of 9 units
items.explanation: A 9-volt battery keeps one terminal 9 volts of 'electrical height' above the other. That voltage difference is what pushes charge through a connected circuit.

items.prompt: The strength of an electric field is often given in 'volts per meter (V/m).' What does that number tell you?
choices: How strong the push is over a given distance
How heavy the charge is
How long the field lasts in seconds
The temperature of the field
items.explanation: Volts per meter is a measure of how strong the field's push is across a stretch of space — a steeper change in voltage over a short distance means a stronger field. No formula is needed; it is just 'how strong the push is.'

items.prompt: A central idea of this course is that a field stores something. What does it store?
choices: Energy
Weight
Sound
Nothing at all
items.explanation: A field stores energy. The space where a field exists is loaded with energy, like a stretched spring. This is why a charged capacitor can release a spark and why waves can carry energy across distances.

items.prompt: Two regions of space contain electric fields. One field is much stronger than the other. Which holds more energy?
choices: The region with the stronger field
The region with the weaker field
They hold exactly the same energy
Neither holds any energy
items.explanation: The stronger the field, the more energy packed into that space. So the region with the stronger field stores more energy.

items.prompt: Why can a charged-up capacitor deliver a spark?
choices: It has stored energy in its electric field, which it can release
It is heavier than the wires around it
It contains a tiny battery of acid
It magnetizes the air
items.explanation: A capacitor stores charge and, with it, energy in the electric field between its parts. Releasing that stored field energy quickly produces a spark. This 'fields hold energy' idea reappears when waves carry energy across space.

items.prompt: Looking ahead: how can an electromagnetic wave carry energy across a room?
choices: It carries fields with it, and fields hold energy
It pushes air molecules the whole way
It uses a hidden wire
It cannot carry energy at all
items.explanation: A wave carries its electric (and magnetic) fields along with it, and those fields store energy. That is how light and radio deliver energy across a room — or across space — with no wire and no air needed.

lessons.title: Magnetism: Magnets and Moving Charges
lessons.content:
You have met electric charge and its field. Now meet its close partner: **magnetism**. You already know it from fridge magnets and the compass that always swings to point north.

**Magnets and poles.** Every magnet has two ends called **poles**: a **north pole** (N) and a **south pole** (S). The rule rhymes with the charge rule from Lesson 1:

- **Like poles repel** — two norths push apart; two souths push apart.
- **Opposite poles attract** — a north and a south pull together.

**The magnetic field.** Just as a charge surrounds itself with an electric field, a magnet surrounds itself with a **magnetic field** — a value at every point telling which way a compass needle would turn. You can *see* this field: sprinkle iron filings around a bar magnet and they line up into curved arcs running from the north pole around to the south pole. Those arcs are magnetic **field lines**. A compass is simply a tiny magnet free to spin; it lines up with the field, which is why it points along Earth's own magnetic field toward the north.

**The big surprise: moving charge makes magnetism.** Here is the discovery that ties the whole subject together. A **moving** electric charge — that is, an electric **current** — creates a magnetic field around it. Run a current through a straight wire and a magnetic field wraps around the wire in circles. (Point your right thumb along the current and your fingers curl the way the field points — a handy 'right-hand' picture, no math needed.) A compass held near a current-carrying wire will swing, just as if a magnet were there.

This is huge: it means electricity and magnetism are not two separate subjects. A *still* charge makes an electric field; a *moving* charge makes a magnetic field too. They are two sides of one thing.

**Recap:** magnets have north and south poles; like poles repel, opposite poles attract. A magnet has a magnetic field you can map with iron filings or a compass. And crucially, a moving charge (a current) creates a magnetic field — the first hint that electricity and magnetism are linked.

items.prompt: What are the two ends of a magnet called?
choices: Positive and negative
North pole and south pole
Top and bottom
Hot and cold
items.explanation: A magnet's two ends are its north pole (N) and south pole (S). Positive and negative are the names for electric charge, not magnetic poles — though the attract/repel rule is similar.

items.prompt: You bring the north poles of two magnets together. What happens?
choices: They pull together (attract)
They push apart (repel)
Nothing happens
One pole turns into a south pole
items.explanation: Like poles repel. Two north poles (or two south poles) push apart. Only a north brought near a south will attract.

items.prompt: Why do iron filings sprinkled around a bar magnet form curved arcs?
choices: The filings are sorting by size
They line up along the magnet's field lines, making the field visible
They are being heated by the magnet
Gravity pulls them into arcs
items.explanation: Each tiny iron filing acts like a little compass and lines up with the magnetic field. The arcs you see are the magnetic field lines made visible, running from the north pole around to the south pole.

items.prompt: How does a compass work?
choices: It is a tiny magnet that lines up with the magnetic field around it
It detects heat from the north
It measures voltage in the air
It points toward the heaviest nearby object
items.explanation: A compass needle is a small magnet free to spin. It lines up with the magnetic field it sits in — normally Earth's field — so it swings to point along that field, roughly toward north.

items.prompt: Besides a magnet, what else can create a magnetic field?
choices: A still, unmoving electric charge
A moving electric charge (an electric current)
A change in temperature
A loud sound
items.explanation: A moving charge — a current — creates a magnetic field. A still charge makes only an electric field; it takes motion to produce magnetism. This is the key link between electricity and magnetism.

items.prompt: Run a steady current through a straight wire. What shape does the magnetic field take around it?
choices: It points straight out from the wire like spokes
It wraps around the wire in circles
It points along the wire in the same direction as the current
There is no magnetic field around a wire
items.explanation: The magnetic field circles around a current-carrying wire. The 'right-hand' picture helps: point your thumb along the current and your fingers curl the way the field wraps around.

items.prompt: You hold a compass next to a wire and then switch on a current. What do you expect?
choices: The compass needle swings, as if a magnet were nearby
The compass needle melts
Nothing, because only magnets affect compasses
The compass points straight down
items.explanation: The current creates a magnetic field around the wire, and the compass (a tiny magnet) responds by swinging. This famous observation showed that electricity and magnetism are connected.

items.prompt: What is a magnetic field, in the same sense as an electric field?
choices: A value at every point in space, telling which way a compass would turn
A single number describing the whole magnet
A kind of electric charge
A beam that only exists in a straight line
items.explanation: Like the electric field, the magnetic field is a value at every point in space — here, the direction and strength that would turn a compass needle. Both are fields: influences spread through the space around their source.

items.prompt: What is the big-picture lesson from 'a still charge makes an electric field, a moving charge also makes a magnetic field'?
choices: Electricity and magnetism are completely unrelated
Electricity and magnetism are two sides of one thing
Magnetism is just heat in disguise
Only magnets, never charges, can make fields
items.explanation: The same charges are behind both fields — still ones make an electric field, moving ones add a magnetic field. So electricity and magnetism are two sides of a single subject, electromagnetism.

lessons.title: The Link, and the Family of Waves
lessons.content:
We end with the idea that pulls everything together and opens the door to the rest of this course: electricity and magnetism are so tightly linked that, working together, they can travel through space as **waves** — the waves we call radio, light, and Wi-Fi.

**The two-way link.** You have already seen one direction: a moving charge (a current) makes a magnetic field. The deep discovery is the reverse as well — a **changing** magnetic field creates an electric field. Put the two together: a changing electric field makes a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes an electric field. Each one, while changing, gives rise to the other. (This back-and-forth is exactly what spins a generator at a power plant: moving magnets near coils create electric currents.)

**The seed of a wave.** Now imagine wiggling a charge back and forth. Its changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field nearby; that changing magnetic field makes a changing electric field a bit farther out; and so on, leapfrogging outward through empty space. That self-propelling ripple of linked electric and magnetic fields is an **electromagnetic wave**. It needs no wire and no air — it carries its own fields, and (from Lesson 3) those fields carry energy. That is how sunlight crosses empty space to reach us.

**One family, many frequencies.** How fast you wiggle the charge sets the wave's **frequency** — how rapidly it oscillates. Remarkably, all of these are the *same* phenomenon, differing only in frequency:

- low frequency: **radio** waves (broadcast, Wi-Fi, microwaves)
- middle: **infrared** (heat) and then **visible light** (the colors we see)
- high frequency: **ultraviolet**, then **X-rays**

Lined up by frequency, they form the **electromagnetic spectrum** — one continuous family. Radio and the light from your lamp are the same kind of thing, just oscillating at wildly different rates.

**Where this is heading.** Everything here has been the plain-words version. The precise rules that make it exact — how a changing electric field makes a magnetic one and vice versa, and exactly how fast the wave travels — are captured in a famous set of statements called **Maxwell's equations**, the subject of the next sub-topic. For now, hold onto the picture: linked, changing fields ripple outward as one family of waves from radio to light.

**Recap:** a changing electric field makes a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes an electric field; together they leapfrog through space as electromagnetic waves. Sorted by frequency they form one spectrum — radio, light, X-rays — and Maxwell's equations (next) make all of this exact.

items.prompt: You already know a moving charge makes a magnetic field. What is the key reverse fact in this lesson?
choices: A changing magnetic field creates an electric field
A still magnet creates an electric current by itself
Magnetism cancels electricity
Nothing can create an electric field
items.explanation: The reverse link is that a changing magnetic field creates an electric field. Combined with 'a changing electric field makes a magnetic field,' this two-way relationship is what allows waves to form.

items.prompt: What is an electromagnetic wave, in plain terms?
choices: A ripple of linked electric and magnetic fields that travels through space
A sound that needs air to move
A wire carrying current
A still magnetic field sitting in place
items.explanation: An electromagnetic wave is a self-propelling ripple of linked, changing electric and magnetic fields moving through space. Each changing field creates the other, so the wave keeps going — no wire or air required.

items.prompt: Why can an electromagnetic wave travel through empty space, like sunlight reaching Earth?
choices: It carries its own electric and magnetic fields, which keep regenerating each other
It rides on air molecules the whole way
It travels along an invisible wire
It is pushed by gravity
items.explanation: The wave carries its own linked fields, each changing field creating the next. Because it needs no medium, it can cross the vacuum of space. Sound, by contrast, needs air or another material.

items.prompt: Radio waves, visible light, and X-rays are best described as:
choices: Three completely different phenomena
The same phenomenon (electromagnetic waves) at different frequencies
Three kinds of sound
Three kinds of electric current
items.explanation: They are all electromagnetic waves; they differ only in frequency. Lined up by frequency they form one continuous family — the electromagnetic spectrum.

items.prompt: Order these from LOW frequency to HIGH frequency.
choices: Radio, visible light, X-rays
X-rays, visible light, radio
Visible light, radio, X-rays
Radio, X-rays, visible light
items.explanation: From low to high frequency: radio waves, then infrared and visible light, then ultraviolet and X-rays. Radio is the low-frequency end; X-rays are near the high-frequency end.

items.prompt: What sets whether a wiggling charge produces, say, a radio wave versus visible light?
choices: How fast the charge wiggles (its frequency)
The color of the wire
The weight of the charge
The time of day
items.explanation: The rate of wiggling sets the wave's frequency, and frequency is what distinguishes radio from light from X-rays. They are otherwise the same kind of wave.

items.prompt: What is the name for the full lineup of electromagnetic waves sorted by frequency?
choices: The electromagnetic spectrum
The periodic table
The sound scale
The voltage ladder
items.explanation: Sorted by frequency, all electromagnetic waves form the electromagnetic spectrum — one continuous family from radio at the low end to X-rays and beyond at the high end.

items.prompt: A power-plant generator makes electric current by:
choices: Moving magnets near coils, so a changing magnetic field drives a current
Heating a wire until it glows
Letting two like charges repel
Standing a magnet perfectly still next to a wire
items.explanation: A generator moves magnets relative to coils, so the magnetic field through the coils keeps changing. A changing magnetic field creates an electric field that drives current — the reverse link in action. A still magnet would do nothing.

items.prompt: This lesson gave the plain-words picture. What makes these ideas exact, and where is it covered?
choices: Maxwell's equations, in the next sub-topic
Newton's laws, covered here
Nothing makes it exact; it stays a rough idea
The periodic table, in chemistry
items.explanation: Maxwell's equations are the precise rules behind the linked fields and the speed of the wave; they are the subject of the next sub-topic. This lesson built the intuition those equations make exact.
